paragraphs.text: "God's help cannot be obstructed by men or demons. Nothing is difficult for God, nor for a Saint. The obstacle for us men .,. is our little faith, by which we prevent the great divine powers from approaching us. "

CHAPTERONE God's Providence ForMan "Seek First the Kingdom of God"' q eronda, Abba Makarios says our God gives us heavenly gifts, 2 and this we believe. Should we believe He will also give us earthly gifts, which are not so important? - What earthly gifts? - Whatever we need. - Yes, you put it well. God !oves His creation, His image, and provides the things which man needs. - Must everyone believe this and not worry? - If one does not believe this and struggles by himselfto acquire these earthly gifts, he will suffer. But even if God does not provide some of these earthly, material things, a person who is leading a spiritual life will not worry about it. If we seek first the Kingdom of God and this is our only concern, all the other things will be given to us, as well. Will God abandon His creation? The manna l. Cf. Mt 6:33.
2. See Saint Makarios of Egypt, Spiritual Homilir:>s, PG 34, 460DA61A and 465D-468A. l

278 DEPEr-iDEJ\CE UPO"' HEA\'EN which God provided for the Israelites in the desert would spoil ifthey kept it for the next day. 3 God arranged things this way so that the people would have confidence in His divine providence.

We have not yet understood the words of Jesus, Seek first the Kingdom of God.4 Either we believe or we don't. When l went to stay at Mount Sinai, I had nothing. But I didn't think at all what would become of me in the desert among strangers, what l would eat, how I would live. The ascetic cell of Saint Episteme, where I was to stay, had been abandoned and uninhabitable for years. As I didn 't want to burden the Monastery of Saint Catherine, l didn't ask for anything. They brought me a little bread from the Monastery, but l returned it.

Why should l be worried, when Christ said, Seekfirst the Kingdom of God. There was also very little water.! had no trade to work and eam my living. The only too] I had was a pair of scissors. I separated it in two, sharpened the two pieces on a stone, took a piece of wood and began making little carved wooden Icons. I worked and said the Jesus Prayer. Quickly I became experienced. l kept making the same design, and the work that would have taken me five days, I could now do in eleven hours, and not only did l help myself, but I also was able to help the little Bedouin children.

For a long time I worked on this task for many hours each day. Later l had reached a point where I did not want to continue with handicraft, but then I could sec the need of the Bedouin children. If you gave them a cap
God"s Providencc ForMan 279 and a pair of sandals, it was a great blessing for them.

So, the thought came to me, "Did l come here to help the Bedouins orto pray for the whole world?' Thus, I decided to stop the work in order to be un-distracted and to pray more. And it wasn 't as ifl was expecting help from somewhere. The Bedouins did not even have enough to eat for themselves. The Monastery was far away. And in the opposite direction it was all desert. But on the same day when I cut back on the work to devote more time to prayer, someone came and found me at my cell and told me, "Here, take these one hundred liras to help the Bedouins without interrupting your program of prayer!" I could not bear this. I left my visitor alone and went into my cell for about fifteen minutes. The providence and the love of God had created such a spiritual state in me that I could not hold back my tears.

Do you see how God provides when there is good will? How much could I have possibly given them? I would give to one child and another would come to remind me, "You didn't give me anything, Father." Then, yet another would come to say the same thing, "F ather, you haven't given me anything!" - Geronda, while many times we have felt the omnipotence of God, why is it that we do not also see His divine providence for us? - This is a trap of the devil. The devil throws ashes in man's eyes so he won't see God's providence. For when man sees the providence of God, his granite heart will soften, become sensitive and burst out in praise of God, and this is not what the devil wants.

280 DEPENDENCE UPON HEAVE'; Man Often Decides Without God Someone had made a tishery and all day long he would express his gratitude, "Glory be to You, O God," for he could see constantly the providence of God. He told me that a tiny tish, from the instant it is fertilized and is as tiny as the head of a pin, it also has a tiny pouch of Ji quid from which it feeds until it grows enough to be able to feed on the micro-organisms in the water. God provides for the tiny fish its own compania! 5 If God provides for the tiny tish, how much more does He provide for man! But man often plans and decides for all things without God. He says, for example, "T am going to have two children." God is not brought into the equation, and so many accidents occur and so many children are lost.
Most people have two children, one is involved in an automobile accident and the other becomes ill and di es, and then they have no children.

When parents, the co-creators with God, encounter difficulties, after all their efforts to settle their children, they should humbly seek the help of the Great Creator by stretching their hands toward Heaven. Then God is pleased and the parents are pleased to be helped. During the time T was in the Monastery of Stomion, I met the head of a family with many children. He was a rural policeman in a village ofEpiros, four anda halfhours on foot from Konitsa, where his family lived. He had nine children. Because the road to the village passed by the Monastery, that policeman would stop by on his way to work and on his way back home. On his way home, he
5. Compania: its own supply offood.

God~s Providence ForMan 281 would ask me to Jet him light the oillamps himself. Even though he would spill some oil, I would allow him to do it, preferring to clean up the tiles of the Church myself later rather than upset him. When he left the Monastery, at about three hundred meters distance, he would tire a shot with his gun. This T could not explain and so I decided to observe him from the moment he entered the Church until he left for Konitsa.

First he would light the lamps in the Church and then would go to the narthex. After lighting the oil-lamp over the entrance in front of the !con of Panaghia, he would dip his finger into the oil of the lamp, go down on his knees, raise his arms toward the Icon and say, "Oh my Panaghia, I have nine children; provide a little meat for them." Then he would rub the oil from his finger on the sight on the barrel of his gun, and leave. Three hundred meters outside the Monastery, where there was a mulberry tree, a wild goat was waiting for him. He would take one shot, as l said, kili the goat, take it down a little way into a cave and skin it, and take it to his children. This would happen every time he returned home from his trip to work. I marvelled at the faith of the rural policeman and the providence of Panaghia.

After twenty-five years he came to find me on the Holy Mountain. At some point I asked him about his children, "How are your children? Where are they?" He then stretched his hand toward the north and said, "Some are in Germany," and then he stretched his hand toward the south and said, "Some are in Australia; glory be to God they are all healthy." That man had preserved his faith and himself pure of any atheistic ideologies, and, for this reason, God was with him and never abandoned him.

282 DEPENDENCE UPON HEAVEN The Blessings o.f Godc~ Amazing Providence - Sometimes, Geronda, I have a wish, and God fulfils it, without me asking Him. How is this done? - God has a way of providing. He sees our needs, our desires, and, when something is for our good, He gives it to us. When someone needs help in something, Christ and Panaghia help. The Elder Philaretos was often asked, "Geronda, what would you like us to provide for you?" And he would answer, "Whatever I want, Panaghia will send it." And that is what would happen.

When we entrust ourselves to God, the Benevolent God looks after us and provides for us. Like a good provider, He gives each of us what is needed, and provides for our material needs even down to the smallest detail. And in order for us to feel His providential care, He gives us exactly what we need. However, you must not expect God to give you first; you must give yourself complete] y to God. For ifyou constantly ask of God, but do not entrust yourself complete] y to God, this implies that you have your own home and are estranged from the eternal heavenly Mansions. Those who give everything to God and entrust themselves to Him dwell under the great dome of God and are protected by His divine providence. Trust in God is a constant secret prayer that quietly brings the powers of God where they are needed and when they are needed, and then His philotimo-filled children constantly praise Him in gratitude.

Father Tychon, when he went to the Kalyvi of the Precious Cross, had no Chape], even though this was essentía] for him. He didn't have any money to build one either; only a strong faith in God. One day he prayed and God"s Providence For Man 283 set out for Karyes, with the faith that God will provide the means needed to build a Chapel. Before arriving at Karyes, the Dikaios 6 of the Skete ofProphet Elias called out to him from a distance. When Father Tychon drew near, the Dikaios told him, "Some good Christian from America sent me this money to give to some ascetic monk who has no Chape l. You don't have a Chape], so take this money and build one." Full of gratitude, Father Tychon was moved to tears for the Benevolent God, Who knows everyone's heart, had already provided for the Chape] and had the money ready to give to him, when he was about to as k for help. 7 When someone abandons himself to God, God does not abandon him. And indeed, if you need something tomorrow at ten o'clock, something that is not irrational but a real need, at nine-forty-five or at nine-thirty, God will have it ready for you. For example, you need a cup at nine o'clock. At five to nine, the cup arrives. You need five hundred drachmas. Just when you need them, five hundred drachmas exactly arrive for you; neither five hundred and ten nor four hundred and ninety. I have observed that if I, for example, need something tomorrow, God has foreseen this from toda y; that is, before I even think of it, God has already thought of it and provides it the moment I need it. For, from where it comes, to get to me exactly when I need it, I can see how much time is needed. Therefore, God has made a provision for it in advance.
6. Thc Elder of a Kalyvi to whom each year the responsibility is given to directa Skete and to loo k after the Kyriakon (the central Church of each Skete) and the pilgrims.
7. See Elder Paisios of Mount Athos, Athonite Fathers and Athonite Matters, p. 29.
284 DEPENDENCE UPON HEAVEN When out ofphilotimo we make God rejoice over our life, then He bestows His blessings abundantly upon His philotimo-filled children, the moment they need them.
After that, all of life flows with the blessings of divine providence. I can go on for hours relating to you examples of God's amazing providence.

When I was in the army, during manoeuvres, I had a New Testament and had given it to someone. Then I would say, "Oh how I wish I had a New Testament, it would be so helpful to me now." At Christmas two hundred packages were sent to us up on the mountain from Mesolonghi. From the two hundred packages only in my package was there a New Testament. It was an old New Testament and had a map of Palestine. In the package was a note saying, "If you need more books, write to us and we' 11 send them." Later, in the Monastery of Stomion, I once needed a vigil oil lamp for the Church. One morning, at sunrise, I went down to Konitsa. As I was passing by a house, I heard a girl say to her father, "Father, the monk is here!" Then he came and told me, "Father Paisios, I have vowed to offer a vigil oil lamp for Panaghia; please take this money and buy one," and handed me five hundred drachmas - exactly the cost of a vigillamp in I 958.

Even now, when I need something, God provides it at once. For example, I need to have some wood cut, but am unable to do it myself. The wood I need is then provided without delay. Before coming here, I received a package with fifty thousand drachmas, exactly the amount I needed. As a blessing I gave someone an !con of Panaghia Axion Estin; the next day someone brought mean Icon of Panaghia Portaitissa.

God,s Providence For Man 285 This year 8 before it rained, I had no water at all. Now that it has rained, I collect a container and a half every day. The cistem has water from last year, but it's in disrepair. But, oh, how God provides! I have one barrel with water. So many people come each day, to drink, to wash and refresh themselves, and yet the leve] only goes down four or five fingers! One hundred fifty to two hundred people are served and the barrel is never empty. Some open the faucet a lot, while others forget to shut it off, and yet the water never ends.

Let Us Entrust Ourselves to Divine Provülence Whoever observes God's benevolence learns to rely upon divine providence. He then feels like an infant in a crib, crying when left alone for a little while by its mother, until she returns to its side. It is an important matter to entrust yourself to God.

When I first went to the Monastery of Stomion, there was no place for me to stay; the Monastery was completely full of rubble. I found a comer near the wall, made a temporary cover and spent the nights there in a sitting position, because there was no room to Ji e down. One day a priest-monk I knew came to me and said, "How can you stay here?" "Why not?" I said, "Did people in the world have any more than what we have? When Konstantinos Kanaris 9 asked for a loan, they told him, 'You have no country.' And he replied, 'We will acquire a country
8. It was spoken in the summer of 1990.
9. Konstantinos Kanaris (1793-1877): Hero of the Greek revolution of 1821, lcgendary fire-ship captain from the heroic island of Psara. He scrvcd as Prime Minister of Greece ( 1864-1865 and 1H77).

286 IJEPENDENCE UPON HEAVEN soon.' Now, if a man in the world had such faith, should we not have trust in God? Since Panaghia provided that I cometo this Monastery, will She not also provide for Her Monastery when the time comes?" And indeed, little by little, Panaghia provided.

I remember when they were pouring the cement to make the floors for the cells they had built, the prepared cement was not enough; they still had to work on a third of the floor. The builders came and told me, "The cement is running out; we'll have to thin it out to make it last for the entire floor." "No," I said to them, "continue to pour it normally." It wasn't possible to bring any more cement at the time, because the animals were away in the valley. The workers would have had to walk two hours to Konitsa and two more hours to the valley and the fields to find the animals. They couldn't have gone and returned in time. Also, the men had other work; they couldn't come back another da y. I noticed they had poured two-thirds of the flooring. I went into the Chapel and said, "Panaghia, what is to happen now? Please help us." Then I went out... - What happened, Geronda? - The flooring was completed and there was extra cement left over. - Did the workers realize it? - How could they not realize it? Sometimes the help of God and Panaghia is very great indeed!
God Utilizes All Things For Good - Geronda, we sometimes begin some work and then so many obstacles arise. How can we understand if the obstacles are from God?
God's Providence ForMan 287 - We must examine ourselves to see if we are at fault.

If we are not at fault, the obstacle is most likely from God for our benefit. For this reason, no one should worry ifthe work was not done, or if it was delayed. Once I was coming clown from the Monastery of Stomion and going to Konitsa for an urgent matter. Ata difficult turn in the road - I called it Golgotha - I met une! e Anastasi, who was known to us in the Monastery, with three loaded animals. The steep terrain and the heavy load had caused their saddles to slip and one animal was in danger of falling off the edge of the road into the ravine. Uncle Anastasi said to me, "God sent you, Father." I helped him unload and reload the animals and put them back on the road and I left him. After continuing on my way for a considerable distance, I reached a point where a mudslide had just taken place, covering about three hundred meters of the road. Trees and rocks had fallen clown into the river. If I had not delayed my journey, I would have been there precisely at the time the mudslide took place. So I said to myself, "Uncle Anastasi, you saved me. God sent you." Christ from high above sees how each one of us acts, and knows how and when to act for our benefit. He knows how and where to guide us, as long as we ask for His help, tell Him our desires and allow Him to put things in order. When I was in the Monastery of Philotheou, I wanted to go to the wilderness; I was thinking of going to a deserted island. I had made arrangements with a boatman to take me there, but finally he never came. This was the dispensation of God, for I was still inexperienced, and would have come to great harm on the deserted island; the demons would have annihilated me.

288 DEPENDE"'CE UPON HEAVEI\ Since I could not go there, I tumed to Katounakia. I loved the wildemess of Katounakia, and I was praying and preparing myself to go there. I wanted to be a disciple of Father Petras, 10 a very Spiritual Father. But something happened that forced me to go to Konitsa and not to Katounakia. One night after Apodeipnon, I had gone into my cell and was praying until late. At about eleven at night, I !ay down to sleep a little. At one thirty, I was awakened by the semantron of the Monastery calling us to the Church. I attempted to get up but it was impossible.

An invisible powerwas keeping me motionless. I realized that something was happening. I was kept motionless on the bed until twelve noon. I could pray, I could think, but I could not move at all. While I was in that state, I saw as if on a television screen on one si de Katounakia and on the other side the Monastery of Stomion in Konitsa. I tumed my eyes wistfully towards Katounakia. A voice then - it was the voice of Panaghia - told me clearly, "You will not go to Katounakia; you will go to the Monastery of Stomion." "But my Panaghia, I asked for the wildemess and You are now sending me into the world?" I heard the same voice again but this time in a stemer tone, "You will go to meet so and so who will help you greatly." Immediately I was released from that invisible bond and my heart was filled with divine Grace.

Later I went and confided this matter to my Spiritual Father. And he told me that this was the will of God. He also advised me not to speak about this event, but to go, saying that for health reasons - I was coughing blood
10. See Elder Paisios of Mount Athos, Athonite Fathers and Athonite Matters, p. 83-93.

God's Providence ForMan at that time - it was necessary forme to leave the Holy Mountain for a while. I wanted one thing, but God had another plan forme. I thought it was God's will for me to go there and help restare the Monastery, and thus fulfil the vow I had made to Panaghia during the war. I had said then, "Panaghia, help me become a monk and I will work for three years to rebuild your burned-out Monastery." As proven later by the events, the main reason for Panaghia sending me there was to help the eighty families who had become Protestants to return to the Orthodox Church.

God will often allow things that are for the benefit of the many to happen. He never does one single good alone, but severa] good things together. Nor does He allow some evil to occur, unless many good things are meant to come out of it. God uses everything for our benefit, including the bad and the dangerous. The good is mixed with the bad; it would have been good if they were separated, but when personal interests enter into the picture, they get mixed. But God can utilize even this mixture of good and bad. This is why we must believe that God allows things to happen, only when good can come out of them, for He !oves His creation. For example, He may allow a small temptation to take place, in order to protect us from a greater one.

Once on the Holy Mountain a layman went to a monastery where they were celebrating their Feast Day. He drank a little wine and got drunk. When he left the monastery, he fell down on his back, and as it was snowing got covered by the snow. His pungent breath had opened a hole in the snow. At some point, a person passed by and saw the hole in the snow. "What's this, a spring?" l
290 DEPENDENCE UPON HEAVEN he thought and poked it with bis walking stick. "Ouch!" called out the drunk man from beneath the snow, and so God provided for this man to be saved.

God:~ Benefactions Break the Heart - Geronda, what is it that God wants from us? - God wants from us our good intentions, our good will, which will be expressed by even our limited but full of philotimo spiritual struggle, and the awareness of our sinfulness. Everything else is given by Him. One doesn 't need muse les in the spirituallife. We must struggle humbly, we must ask for God 's merey and we must be grateful to Him for everything. He who abandons himself into the hands of God, without any plan of his own, will pass into God's plan. As long as man is hooked onto himself, he remains behind; he does not progress spiritually because he obstructs God's merey. In order to grow spiritually he must place great trust in God.

At all times God caresses the hearts of all the people with His love, but we are not aware of it because our hearts have gathered a crust. When man has cleansed bis heart, he is deeply moved and madly excited, because he can then see the benefactions, the blessings of God, Who !oves all people in the same way. For those who are suffering, God is pained; for those who are living a spiritual life, He rejoices. When a phi/otimo-filled soul thinks of God 's blessings, it is enough to send it exploding toward Heaven, even more so if the soul also meditates on its many sins and God's abundant merey and compassion!

When man becomes aware of God's providential care, and the eyes of bis soul have been purified, he can exGod's Providcnce ForMan 291 perience and live the fullness of divine providence with bis cleansed heart, which is then overwhelmed by a profound sense of gratitude and spiritual madness, in the good sense of the term. For God's blessings, when man is aware of them, create a crack in bis heart, and break it. Then, as the hand of God caresses bis phi/otimo-filled heart and touches the crack, man experiences an inner explosion that increases bis gratitude toward God. All those who struggle and are aware of their sinfulness and God's blessings, entrusting themselves to His abundant merey, elevate their soul to Paradise with great certainty and less bodily effort.

Gratitude to God For Little and For Much Some people say, "I believe God will help me," and at the same time they try to make lots of money so that they won 't be deprived of anything. These people mock God, because they do not entrust themselves to God, but to money. If they don't stop loving money and placing their hope in it, they won 't be able to establish their bope in God. I'm not saying that people sbouldn't put some money aside for a time of need; l'm saying tbat tbey sbouldn't set tbeir bopes on money and set tbeir beart on it, because tbis way tbey forget God. Wboever makes bis own plans, witbout putting bis trust in God, and tben says tbat tbis is bow God wants it, is actually blessing bis own work in a demonic way and is constantly tormented. We bave not understood tbe power and goodness of God. We do not allow Him as Lord to govern us, and tbis is wby we are tormented.

292 DEPENDENCE UPON HEAVEN At Mount Sinai, in the ascetic cell of Saint Episteme where I lived, water was scarce. It dripped from a rock in a cave, some twenty meters from the cell. I had made a little cistern and gathered about three kilos of water every twenty-four hours. When I went to fetch the water, I dipped my tin cup in the cistern to fill it while reciting the Salutations to the Theotokos. With my hand, I would wet my brow a little, as a doctor had advised me, to help me. Then I would take a little water to have for drinking.

I also took some water in a small can for the birds and the mice that lived in the ascetic ce!!. With this same water, I would al so wash some clothing, and so forth. What joy, what gratitude I experienced for that small amount ofwater I had. I sang Doxologies for the water I had.

When I came to the Holy Mountain and stayed for a while at the Skete ofiveron Monastery, the place was sunny and had plenty of water. There was a cistern that filled up and the water overflowed. Here I could wash my feet and my head, but I had become forgetful of God. At Sinai, my eyes filled with tears of gratitude for the small amount of water, but at the Skete I became forgetful of God because of the abundance ofwater. For this reason, I went and lived some eighty meters away and had a small cistern. How one can lose himself, how one can become forgetful with an abundance of even ordinary material things!

We must unreservedly surrender ourselves to God's providence, to the divine will, and God will take care of us. A monk went one afternoon to read the Vespers Service on a hilltop. On the way there, he found a white mushroom and thanked God for the rare find. Upon returning, he would cut it and get through the evening with that. In God's Providence ForMan 293 the meantime, he thought to himself, "If laypeople ask me if I eat meat, I can tell them that I do every autumn." Upon his return, he found the mushroom to be halved in size- some animal must have stepped on it- and he said, "It seems that this is all I was supposed to eat." He picked it and thanked God for His providence, for the half mushroom. Further down he found another halfmushroom and reached down to pick it, wanting to complete his evening mea!, but because it seemed spoiled - perhaps even poisonous - he left it and thanked God again for saving him from being poisoned. He went to his Kalyvi and spent the night with half a mushroom for food. The next day, when he came out of his Kalyvi, he saw a wonderful sight! The whole area was filled with beautiful mushrooms, and he thanked God. You see, he thanked God for the whole mushroom and for the half mushroom, for the good one and for the spoiled one, for the one and for the many. He gave thanks for everything.

The Benevolent God provides an abundance ofblessings and always acts for our good. All the good things we have are gifts from God. He has made everything to serve His creation, the human being, and to be sacrificed for him, animals and birds, small and large, even plants, - God, too, sacrificed Himself to redeem man. Let us not be indifferent to all His benevolence and cause Him grief through our great insensitivity and ingratitude, but, rather, Jet us thank Him and praise Him.

CHAPTERTWO Faith and Trust in God ' 1 · ¡ '' We Must Believe in God With Philotimo (j eronda, I am troubled by the thoughts ofunbelief that come to me. - The fact that you are troubled by them and do not accept them, means that these thoughts come from the evil one. Sometimes Christ allows thoughts of doubt or unbelief in order to test our disposition and our philotimo, for our God is not a myth like Zeus, Apollo, but a real and living God, and our faith is a true and living faith.

We have a cloud of witnesses in the Saints, as Saint Paul says, I who knew Christ, who lived with Him and who sacrificed themselves for Him. And in our own present time, there are people devoted to God who have heavenly experiences and are in communion with the Angels, the Saints and even with Christ and Panaghia. I'm going to share something from my own life to help you. You see, I, too, can be ... a "blood donor"; I reveal certain events to be helpful. Where I see heaped-up knowledge and l. Cf. Heb 12:1.
Faith and Trust in God 295 neglected faith, I want to strengthen the faith by relating some events and examples of faith.

When I was a young boy in Konitsa, I read about the lives of many Saints, and sometimes I gave the books to the other boys to read, or I would gather them in one place and we would read together. I marvelled at the great degree of asceticism and the fasting that the Saints did, and tried to do the same. From fasting, my neck had become as thin as a cherry stem. The children teased me.

''Your head wi 11 fall of," they would say to me. What I had to put up with! Well, never mind. In addition, because I often got sick from fasting, my older brother was afraid that I would not be able to finish school, so he would take and hide my little pamphlets with the lives of the Saints. After that, I started hiding them in the forest, at the country Chapel of Saint Barbara, and would go there secretly to read them.

One day a neighbour named Kostas said to my brother, "I will make him change his way of thinking, throw away those booklets he is reading, and give up his fasting and prayer." He found me -I was about fifteen years o id then- and he started talking to me about Darwin 's theory.

He started talking, and went on and on and was making my head spin. In my haze, I headed for the forest, to the Chapel of Saint Barbara. I went in and began praying to Christ. "My Christ, if You exist, reveal Yourselfto me in some way," I kept saying, while constantly doing prostrations for a long time. It was summer. The sweat was running down my body, and I was drenched and completely exhausted. But I didn't hear or see anything. Nor did God help me in any way with even a small sign, some sound, some shadow; I was only a child, after all. Even if one

296 DEPENDENCE UPON HEAVEN were to \ook at this from a human and logical perspective, one could say, "My God, what a shame for the poor child. From eleven years old, he has been going up to the hills to live ascetically, and now he is going through a crisis. That man made his head spin with some foolish theories. At home, he has difficu\ties with his brother. He ran to the forest to seek help from You ... " And yet nothing, nothing, nothing! Exhausted from the many prostrations, I sat down for a while. Then l thought, "Al\ right, when l asked Kostas what he thought about Christ, what did he te\\ me? He told me that Christ was the best, the most righteous man Who proclaimed righteousness and had so offended the Pharisees that they crucified Him out of envy." Then l said, "If Christ was such a good man, so righteous, and no other man had ever appeared like Him, and others killed Him out of envy, then it is right of meto do for this man far more than I have done, evento die for him." As soon as llooked at it this way, Christ appeared to me in a great light- the Chapel was fu\\ of light- and told me, I am the Resurrection and the Lije. He that be/ieveth in me, though he die, yet sha/I he live. 2 These same words I could also read in the open Gospel which He held in one hand. There was such an inner transformation in me that l found myself saying constantly, "Come here now, Kosta, to discuss if there is or there isn't a God." You see, for Christ to appear He waited for my philotimofilled response. Now, if Christ requires such a response from even a young child, can you imagine how much more He would expect from a mature adult?
